title: 11+ Interview Preparation Guide
subtitle: Top Independent Schools — Discussion & General Knowledge Questions

sections.partDescription: These questions have no right answer. Interviewers want to see curiosity, reasoning, and the ability to consider other points of view.

questions: Have you been following anything in the news recently? What did you make of it?
Do you think social media does more good or more harm for people your age?
Do you think richer countries have a responsibility to help poorer ones?
If you had to pick one problem in the world to solve, what would it be and why?
What do you think about AI — is it something to be excited about or worried about?
If you used AI to help write an essay, would you consider that cheating?
Do you think a computer could ever be truly creative — like write a poem that actually means something?
Is there a job you think a robot could never do? Why?
Who do you think should be doing the most to tackle climate change — governments, businesses, or individuals?
Is it fair that your generation has to deal with a problem it didn't really create?
Is there something you'd genuinely be willing to give up to help the environment?
Say you found £50 on the pavement and there was no one around — what would you do?
Can you think of a situation where lying might actually be the right thing to do?
If you saw someone being bullied and stepping in felt risky, what would you do?
If you could only save five strangers or one close friend, what would you do?
Do you think school uniforms are a good idea?
Is it fair that some people are born into wealthy families and others aren't?
Should 16-year-olds be allowed to vote? Why or why not?
If you saw someone in your class being left out or picked on, what would you do?
Have you ever had a falling out with a friend? How did you handle it, and would you do anything differently now?
If your closest friend did something you thought was really wrong, would you say something to them?
Say two of your friends had an argument and both came to you separately expecting you to take their side — how would you deal with that?

topics.topic: Personal Curiosity & Interests Outside the Classroom
questions: Is there a book you've read that made you see something differently?
What's something you've gone and looked into just because you wanted to know more about it?
Has there been something you used to believe that you've since changed your mind on? What changed it?
Is there a sport, hobby or activity you do outside of school that you're really passionate about?
What's something you're good at that you've had to work hard to get better at?
Have you ever taken on a leadership role — in a team, a club, or even just among friends? What did you learn from it?
Is there a skill you've taught yourself, outside of school? How did you go about learning it?
What do you do when you have completely free time and no one is telling you what to do?
Has anything you've done outside school — a trip, a competition, a project — changed the way you think about something?

sections.partDescription: Answers are provided for each question.

questions.question: What is the name of the UK's Parliament building?
What are the two houses of the UK Parliament called?
options: The Upper House and the Lower Council

questions.question: At what age can you vote in a UK general election?
What does "democracy" mean?
options: A country ruled by one person with all the power
A country where the army makes all the decisions
A system where people vote to choose who runs the country
A country run by the wealthiest people
questions.answer: A system where people vote to choose who runs the country

questions.question: Which political party won the most recent UK general election (2024)?
How many countries make up the United Kingdom?
What does the term "refugee" mean?
options: Someone who moves abroad to find a better-paid job
Someone who is on holiday in another country
Someone who has fled their home country because of war, danger or persecution
Someone who has been sent abroad by their employer
questions.answer: Someone who has fled their home country because of war, danger or persecution

questions.question: Russia invaded which neighbouring country in 2022, causing a major war in Europe?
Which country has the largest population in the world?
What gas, released by burning oil, coal and gas, is most responsible for global warming?
Which of the following is a renewable energy source?
What is deforestation?
options: Cutting down large areas of forest, often for farming or building
A method of protecting forests from fire
questions.answer: Cutting down large areas of forest, often for farming or building

questions.question: Which of the following is the best example of AI being used in real life?
options: A traffic light that changes on a timer
A streaming service recommending shows based on what you've already watched
A light bulb switching on automatically at sunset
questions.answer: A streaming service recommending shows based on what you've already watched

questions.question: What is "misinformation"?
options: Information that is hard to understand
Information that is deliberately kept secret
False or inaccurate information that spreads quickly, often online
questions.answer: False or inaccurate information that spreads quickly, often online

questions.question: Which of these is considered a basic human right?
Which of the following best describes what a charity does?
options: It sells products to make as much profit as possible
It is run by the government to collect taxes
It raises money to help people, animals or causes without making a profit
questions.answer: It raises money to help people, animals or causes without making a profit

tip: For discussion questions, always give a reason for your answer. If the interviewer challenges you, don't immediately back down — think it through and explain your reasoning. It's fine to say "I hadn't thought of it that way, but I still think..." or "That's a good point, maybe I'd change my view on that because..."
